title: Green tomato chutney
subtitle: End-of-season tomatoes slowly cooked down with vinegar and spice.
excerpt: Unripe tomatoes from the end of the garden season turned into a dark, sweet-sharp chutney with cider vinegar, brown sugar, apple and warm spices. Ready to eat after two weeks and better after two months. Makes about 4 jars.
sourceNotes: Green tomato chutney. Chutney in the British tradition derives from Indian achar preserved in vinegar and adapted for the Victorian larder. Green tomato chutney appears in late Victorian and Edwardian domestic cookery books as a means of preserving tomatoes that have not ripened before the first frosts. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861, public domain) documents vinegar-preserved relishes of this type.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for 12 months. Leave for at least 2 weeks before opening for the flavours to mellow.

content.text: Green tomato chutney is an autumn preserve by nature: you make it when the plants stop ripening and there is a glut of hard, tart, unripe fruit. The long slow cook over two hours reduces everything to a thick, jammy relish. Resist opening the jar for at least two weeks; the vinegar edge needs time to mellow.

Put {{tomato}} of green tomatoes, {{apple-bramley}} of chopped apple, {{onion}} of diced onion, {{dark-brown-sugar}} of sugar, {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar, {{mustard-seeds-yellow}} of mustard seeds, {{ginger-ground}} of ground ginger, {{turmeric}} of turmeric and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt into a large heavy pan. Bring to a gentle simmer, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Cook uncovered over low heat for 1.5 to 2 hours, stirring regularly, until the chutney is thick enough that a wooden spoon drawn across the bottom of the pan leaves a clear trail. Ladle into sterilised jars and seal immediately.

items.symptom: Chutney too sharp straight from the jar
items.fix: Two weeks minimum; two months is better. The vinegar acid mellows significantly as it sits.

items.symptom: Chutney sticks to the bottom and scorches
items.cause: Heat too high, or insufficient stirring towards the end.
items.fix: Reduce heat to the lowest setting for the last 30 minutes and stir every few minutes.

items.cause: Insufficient cooking time; not reduced enough.
items.fix: Return to the heat and continue cooking uncovered. The trail test is the best guide.

content.text: Green tomato chutney is a garden preserve, made when summer ends and the tomatoes will not ripen in time. It belongs to the British tradition of frugal preserving: nothing is wasted. Serve alongside cold meats, in a ploughman's lunch, or on a cheese sandwich. The brown, deeply-flavoured result looks nothing like the fruit it started with, and tastes better for it.
